what_it_does: Runs a drift acceptance test that compares discovered runtime capabilities against the maintained MAP.md registry block and fails if any node-type, RHM verb, mode, panel, or acceptance suite is missing.
observations: Sets up a temporary FsStore, discovers nodes via NodeRegistry, and creates a Suite pointed at the real nodes directory so it reads the real MAP.md.
Defines a global PASS counter and a check(label, cond) helper that asserts, increments, and prints.
Calls suite.doc_drift() and verifies five drift categories are empty.
Includes a prose-guard note that words appearing only in MAP.md prose must not satisfy the registry-block check.
Uses sys.path insertion to import project modules from the parent directory.
standouts: Treats MAP.md as a tested artifact rather than free-form documentation.
Block-scoped drift check explicitly excludes whole-file prose to avoid false positives.
Fails loudly on any missing registry entry for a registered capability.
conventions: Follows a simple assertion-based test script style.
Uses sys.path manipulation to make local package imports work.
Breaks typical pytest/unit-test conventions by using global script-level execution and mutable global state.
concerns: Global mutable PASS counter and check side effects make the script non-reentrant.
Temporary store directory created with mkdtemp is not explicitly cleaned up.
Test outcome depends on the real MAP.md and nodes directory, so it is environment-sensitive.
No function-level docstring or type hints for check.
notes: A self-checking acceptance script that asserts the project's MAP.md registry block stays synchronized with discovered node types, verbs, modes, panels, and suites.
questions: What is the exact syntax of the MAP.md registry blocks that doc_drift() scans?
How does Suite.doc_drift() distinguish prose paragraphs from registry blocks?
What are the concrete definitions of RHM verbs, modes, panels, and acceptance suites?
Why is 'right-hand-man' chosen as the specific prose-guard example?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose—making documentation drift fail loudly by keeping MAP.md synchronized with registered capabilities—matches the actual checks performed.
apparent_themes: Fail-loud prevention of silent documentation rot.
Block-scoped verification rather than whole-file text matching.
intention_signals: It is trying to enforce a social/technical contract: any new capability added to the runtime must also be recorded in the maintained MAP.md registry block, so the map remains authoritative.
novelty: It treats a markdown registry block as a tested artifact, using a dedicated drift report to surface out-of-sync documentation.
contribution: Provides an automated guard that surfaces when implemented node types, RHM verbs, modes, panels, or acceptance suites are absent from the MAP.md registry block, turning documentation drift into a visible test failure.
summary_for_embedding: drift_acceptance.py is a standalone acceptance test that instantiates FsStore, NodeRegistry, and Suite, calls Suite.doc_drift(), and asserts that the real MAP.md registry block reflects every discovered node-type, RHM verb, mode, panel, and acceptance suite.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to make documentation rot fail loudly by enforcing that the maintained MAP.md registry block stays in sync with all registered system capabilities.

symbols.does: Asserts a condition, increments PASS on success, prints an ok message, and aborts with a FAIL label on assertion failure.
symbols.description: A small inline assertion helper used by the drift acceptance script. It takes a human-readable label and a boolean condition, asserts the condition, increments the global PASS counter, and prints a success line. If the condition is false it raises AssertionError with a 'FAIL: <label>' message, halting the script.
